articles.title: Movies Anywhere Adds Lionsgate, Still Missing Big Studios
articles.summary: Lionsgate has joined the Movies Anywhere digital film platform, expanding consumer access to their owned titles. However, major studios like Paramount and MGM remain notable holdouts, preventing a truly unified digital movie library for consumers. This fragmented landscape continues to frustrate viewers who want seamless access to their purchased content across platforms, highlighting the ongoing struggle for interoperability and content portability in the competitive and often walled-garden world of digital media.

articles.title: Tech Stars of the 90s Reborn on AI Hype
articles.summary: Unexpectedly, iconic tech companies from the 1990s, including Nokia, Dell, and Cisco, are experiencing a significant resurgence, buoyed by the insatiable demand for AI infrastructure. These 'legacy tech' giants, with their established hardware, networking capabilities, and enterprise solutions, are proving crucial enablers for AI deployment, rather than casualties of disruption. This demonstrates how foundational, often overlooked, technologies are becoming critically valuable in the age of advanced computing, challenging the narrative of continuous, absolute disruption.

Their stock values are surging as investors recognize their critical role in AI infrastructure.
articles.why_matters: If you're an investor, don't overlook 'boring' infrastructure tech—they're the picks and shovels of the AI gold rush. This trend shows the AI revolution isn't just about new startups; it's also about upgrading existing massive IT infrastructure. For CIOs, relying on established vendors like Cisco and Dell for AI infrastructure offers stability and proven scale. Expect these companies to continue investing heavily in R&D to adapt their offerings to AI-specific demands. This could lead to a less fragmented and more integrated AI ecosystem, as dominant players consolidate.

articles.title: Expert: Anyone Can Build a Platform, Few Get Eyeballs
articles.summary: A tech expert highlights a critical disconnect in the digital economy: while advances in open-source tools and cloud infrastructure have democratized platform creation, attracting users remains an insurmountable hurdle for most. The proliferation of digital services makes it easy to 'build it,' but exceptionally difficult to ensure anyone 'comes.' This underscores that the real competition isn't in technology development, but in attention economics, influencing content discoverability, marketing spend, and the dominance of established tech giants.
articles.deck: A tech expert states platform creation is easy, but audience attainment remains the challenge.

Attention economics is now the primary battleground, overshadowing technological barriers.
This explains the continued dominance of established tech giants in user acquisition.
articles.why_matters: If you're an aspiring entrepreneur, this means your brilliant tech idea is useless without a rock-solid, multi-million dollar audience acquisition strategy. This explains why big tech companies continue to dominate: they own the distribution channels and user attention. Expect rising costs for digital marketing and advertising as competition for user eyeballs intensifies. This pushes innovation towards discoverability mechanisms and network effects rather than just core product features. For content creators, getting noticed on crowded platforms requires increasingly niche focus and savvy self-promotion.

articles.title: DroneAcharya Reports Profit Despite 57% Revenue Slump
articles.summary: DroneAcharya, a prominent drone services provider, announced a net profit of Rs 37 lakh for FY26, a surprising result given its substantial 57% revenue slump. This counter-intuitive outcome suggests an aggressive cost-cutting strategy and operational realignment to achieve profitability amidst challenging market conditions, underscoring the volatile, yet rapidly evolving, economic landscape for companies in the rapidly expanding drone industry.

articles.why_matters: If you're an investor in the drone sector, this signals that profitability is achievable, but often through severe cost-cutting rather than pure growth. Market consolidation is likely: smaller, less efficient drone companies will struggle to survive major revenue dips. Competitive pressure means drone service providers must optimize operations intensely to stay afloat and thrive. Emerging tech sectors often experience this 'shake-out' phase; expect more strategic pivots and failures before stability.
